title: Object Pronouns and Their Combinations
description: Replace nouns with direct (lo, la, los, las) and indirect (le, les) object pronouns, place them correctly (before a conjugated verb, attached to infinitives, gerunds and commands), and combine two pronouns including the key le/les -> se change (Se lo doy).

cards.front: me, te, lo/la, nos, os, los/las
cards.back: direct object pronouns (the 'it / him / her / them' that receives the action)
cards.notes: **Direct object** pronouns replace the thing or person directly acted on: *¿Ves la casa? La veo.* (Do you see the house? I see **it**.) The 3rd person agrees in gender and number: **lo** (masc.), **la** (fem.), **los/las** (plural).

cards.front: me, te, le, nos, os, les
cards.notes: **Indirect object** pronouns mark the person who receives *to* or *for* whom something is done: *Le doy el libro* = I give the book **to him/her**. Note 3rd person is **le / les** (no gender change), unlike the direct *lo/la*.

cards.front: Lo veo. / La conozco. / Te llamo.
cards.back: pronouns go BEFORE a conjugated verb
cards.notes: English puts the object **after** the verb (*I see **it***). Spanish puts the pronoun **before** the conjugated verb: *Lo veo.* Never *Veo lo*. This reversed word order is the #1 thing to retrain.

cards.front: Quiero verlo. / Voy a verlo. / Estoy haciéndolo.
cards.back: pronouns attach to infinitives and gerunds
cards.notes: With an **infinitive** (*verlo*) or **gerund** (*haciéndolo*) the pronoun attaches to the end. With two verbs you may choose: *Lo quiero ver* OR *Quiero verlo* — both correct. Attaching to a gerund adds a written accent (*haciéndolo*).

cards.back: attach to affirmative commands; before negative ones
cards.notes: **Affirmative** commands take the pronoun attached, often adding an accent: *Cómpralo* (Buy it). **Negative** commands put it before: *No lo compres* (Don't buy it). Same split for *Hazlo* vs *No lo hagas*.

cards.front: Me lo da. / Te la doy. / Nos los trae.
cards.notes: When both appear together, the **indirect** pronoun comes first, then the **direct**: *Me lo da* = He gives **it to me**. Order: indirect + direct + verb. English says it in the opposite order ('it to me'), so the sequence feels reversed.

cards.front: Se lo doy.  (NOT le lo doy)
cards.notes: The big rule: when **le** or **les** would sit next to **lo, la, los, las**, it changes to **se**. *Le doy el libro -> Se lo doy.* You can never say *le lo*. There is **no English parallel**, so this must be memorized.

cards.front: A Juan le doy el libro. / A ella le gusta.
cards.notes: Spanish often **doubles** the indirect object: the pronoun *le* PLUS *a + person*. *A Juan le doy el libro* (literally 'to Juan I give him the book'). It clarifies who *le/se* refers to, since *se lo doy* alone could mean to him, her, them or you.

cards.front: el coche -> lo veo / la casa -> la veo
cards.back: lo/la must agree with the noun's gender
cards.notes: Choose the direct pronoun by the gender of the noun it replaces: *el coche* (masc.) -> **lo** veo; *la casa* (fem.) -> **la** veo; *los libros* -> **los**; *las llaves* -> **las**. English 'it' has no gender, so this agreement is easy to forget.

cards.front: Me gusta el café. / Le interesa el arte.
cards.notes: Verbs like *gustar, encantar, interesar* always use an **indirect** pronoun: *Me gusta* = it is pleasing **to me**. The thing that pleases is the subject and controls the verb: *Me gustan los libros* (plural).

cards.back: leísmo: using 'le' for a male person (Spain)
cards.notes: In much of Spain, *le* is used as the **direct** object for a male person: *A Pablo le vi ayer* instead of *lo vi*. This is called **leísmo** and is accepted for male people. Standard / Latin American Spanish prefers *lo*.

cards.back: don't drop the pronoun in short answers
cards.notes: English can drop the object (*Yes, I have*). Spanish keeps the pronoun: *¿Tienes el billete? Sí, lo tengo.* Leaving it out (*Sí, tengo*) sounds incomplete. The pronoun stands in for the noun you already mentioned.

steps.title: Two kinds of object pronouns
steps.body:
# Direct and indirect object pronouns

A pronoun lets you avoid repeating a noun. Spanish has two sets.

**Direct object** = the thing or person directly acted on (the 'what' or 'whom'):

| Person | DO pronoun |
|---|---|
| me | me |
| you | te |
| him/her/it | **lo / la** |
| us | nos |
| you (pl.) | os |
| them | **los / las** |

*¿Ves la casa? — Sí, **la** veo.* (Do you see the house? Yes, I see **it**.)

**Indirect object** = the person *to/for whom* (the receiver):

| Person | IO pronoun |
|---|---|
| to me | me |
| to you | te |
| to him/her | **le** |
| to us | nos |
| to you (pl.) | os |
| to them | **les** |

*¿El libro? — **Le** doy el libro a María.* (I give the book **to her**.)

> Note the only real difference is the 3rd person: direct **lo/la/los/las** (gender!) vs indirect **le/les** (no gender).

steps.title: Where the pronoun goes
steps.body:
## Position: this is where English-speakers slip

English puts the object **after** the verb: *I see **it**.* Spanish puts the pronoun **before** the conjugated verb:

> *Lo veo.* (not *Veo lo*)
> *Te llamo mañana.*

**But** with an infinitive, a gerund, or an affirmative command, it **attaches to the end**:

- Infinitive: *Quiero **verlo**.* / *Voy a **verlo**.* (also fine: *Lo quiero ver* / *Lo voy a ver*)
- Gerund: *Estoy **haciéndolo**.* (note the accent)
- Affirmative command: *Cómpra**lo**.* / *Dá**me**lo.*

**Negative** commands flip back to before: *No **lo** compres.* / *No **lo** hagas.*

> ⚠️ The order *Veo lo* / *Llamo te* is always wrong. Retrain the reflex: pronoun first, then the conjugated verb.

steps.title: Combining two pronouns
steps.body:
## Indirect before direct — and le -> se

When both pronouns appear, the **indirect** comes first, then the **direct**, then the verb:

> *Me lo da.* = He gives **it to me**.
> *Te la traigo.* = I bring **it to you**.

**The key rule:** when *le* or *les* would meet *lo, la, los, las*, it changes to **se** — you can never say *le lo*:

> *Le doy el libro* -> ***Se** lo doy.* (NOT *le lo doy*)
> *Les compro los billetes* -> ***Se** los compro.*

There is **no English equivalent** for this change, so memorize it.

**Clarifying 'a + person'.** Because *se* (and *le*) is ambiguous, Spanish often adds *a + person*, and even doubles it with a noun already present:

> *A Juan **le** doy el libro.* / *Se lo doy **a ella**.*

> Regional note: in much of Spain, **leísmo** uses *le* as the direct object for a male person (*A Pablo le vi* = lo vi). It is accepted; Latin American Spanish prefers *lo*.

exercise.prompt: Match each noun with the direct object pronoun that replaces it.
Match each English phrase with its indirect object pronoun.
Translate: *I see it.* (referring to *el coche*, the car)
exercise.hint: el coche is masculine -> lo, and the pronoun goes BEFORE the verb.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the indirect pronoun. Remember what happens before *lo*.
exercise.sentence: ¿El libro? ___ lo doy a María mañana.
exercise.hint: le would meet lo -> it must change. le/les -> se.

steps.title: Build: He gives it to me

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *He gives it to me.* (it = el dinero)
exercise.hint: Indirect (me) before direct (lo), then the verb.

steps.title: Build: I want to see it

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I want to see it.* (it = la película) Attach the pronoun to the infinitive.
exercise.hint: With an infinitive the pronoun attaches to the end: ver + la = verla.

exercise.prompt: You tell a friend to buy it (it = el pan). Which sentence is the correct affirmative command 'Buy it'?
exercise.hint: Affirmative commands attach the pronoun and often take an accent: Cómpralo.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I give it to her.* (it = el libro)
exercise.hint: le + lo is impossible -> le becomes se. Indirect (se) before direct (lo).

exercise.prompt: Fill in the indirect pronoun that doubles 'a Juan'.
exercise.hint: Spanish doubles the indirect object: a Juan + the matching pronoun (singular, to him).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *Yes, I have it.* (it = el billete, the ticket)
exercise.hint: el billete is masculine -> lo. Keep the pronoun; don't say 'Sí, tengo'.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the indirect pronoun: 'I like coffee' (literally 'coffee is pleasing to me').
exercise.hint: gustar uses an indirect pronoun: to me = me.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *They bring them to us.* (them = los paquetes, the packages)
exercise.hint: Indirect (nos = to us) before direct (los = them, masc. plural), then the verb traen.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish sentence with its English meaning.
